---
title: "Ch 10a тАФ Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)"
subtitle: "Where you slice a cone, a circle and a parabola fall out тАФ and their equations follow"
lesson-id: "cbse11.conic-sections-circle-parabola"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 10
ncert-chapter-title: "Conic Sections"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse11.straight-lines", "cbse10.quadratic-equations"]
unlocks: ["cbse11.conic-sections-ellipse-hyperbola"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you state the distance formula in the plane?
2. Are you fluent with completing the square on a quadratic?
3. Can you sketch $y = x^2$ from memory?
4. Have you heard the words *focus* and *directrix*, even informally?

Three confident yeses тАФ read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a flashlight and shine it at a wall. Tilt the flashlight slowly.

- Pointed straight at the wall: the bright region is a **circle**.
- Tilted slightly: the boundary becomes an **ellipse** (you'll see this in
  Lesson 10b).
- Tilted until one edge of the cone is parallel to the wall: a
  **parabola**.
- Tilted further: a **hyperbola** (10b again).

All four shapes are *slices of a cone*. Today we focus on the circle and
the parabola.

The widget below shows a parabola $y = x^2/(4p)$ with focus
$(0, p)$ and directrix $y = -p$. Drag $p$ and watch the curve open.

```{ojs}
viewof pVal = Inputs.range([0.5, 4], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "p (focus distance)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const p = pVal;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Parabola:</b> y┬▓ = 4p x with focus at (p, 0), directrix x = тИТp.</div>
    <div>Here p = ${p.toFixed(2)}, so equation: <b>y┬▓ = ${(4 * p).toFixed(2)} x</b>.</div>
    <div>Focus: <b>(${p.toFixed(2)}, 0)</b>, directrix: <b>x = ${(-p).toFixed(2)}</b>.</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      Every point on this parabola is equidistant from the focus and the directrix.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The parabola is *not* "the graph of $y = x^2$ that I drew in Class 10".
It is the curve of points that are *equidistant from a fixed point and
a fixed line*.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. A circle is "all points at distance $r$ from a centre". Can you turn
   that sentence into an equation?
2. A parabola is "all points equidistant from a focus and a directrix".
   Why does that lead to a *curve*, not a single point or a line?
3. Light arriving parallel to the axis of a parabola, after reflecting,
   passes through *one* point. Which?
4. The equation $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ тАФ is the centre at the origin?
   What if you had $(x - 3)^2 + (y + 4)^2 = 25$ instead?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(3) is why parabolic dishes (satellite, telescope, headlight) work.
(4) is the **standard form vs general form** issue тАФ completing the
square shifts between them.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 тАФ Equation of a circle

Find the equation of the circle with centre $(2, -3)$ and radius $5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Distance from $(x, y)$ to $(2, -3)$ equals $5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x - 2)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 25$.

Expanded: $x^2 - 4x + 4 + y^2 + 6y + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 12 = 0$.
</details>

### Problem 2 тАФ General to standard

Find the centre and radius of $x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 8y - 11 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Complete the square in $x$ and $y$ separately.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x^2 - 6x) + (y^2 + 8y) = 11$
$\Rightarrow (x - 3)^2 - 9 + (y + 4)^2 - 16 = 11$
$\Rightarrow (x - 3)^2 + (y + 4)^2 = 36$.

Centre $(3, -4)$, radius $6$.
</details>

### Problem 3 тАФ Circle through three points

Find the equation of the circle through $(0, 0), (4, 0), (0, 6)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
General circle: $x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0$. Substitute each point.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At $(0,0)$: $c = 0$.

At $(4, 0)$: $16 + 8g = 0 \Rightarrow g = -2$.

At $(0, 6)$: $36 + 12f = 0 \Rightarrow f = -3$.

Equation: $x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y = 0$. Centre $(2, 3)$, radius
$\sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}$.
</details>

### Problem 4 тАФ Standard parabola

Sketch $y^2 = 8x$. Find the focus and directrix.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Compare with $y^2 = 4px$. Then $p$ identifies the focus and directrix.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4p = 8 \Rightarrow p = 2$. Focus $(2, 0)$, directrix $x = -2$. Opens to
the right.
</details>

### Problem 5 тАФ Parabola from focus

Find the equation of the parabola with focus $(0, 3)$ and directrix
$y = -3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Vertex at the midpoint of "focus to directrix" = $(0, 0)$. Axis along
the $y$-axis.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 = 4 \cdot 3 \cdot y = 12y$.
</details>

### Problem 6 тАФ Tangent to a circle

Find the equation of the tangent to $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ at the point $(3, 4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Tangent to $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ at $(x_1, y_1)$ is $x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x + 4y = 25$.
</details>

You have just used the **standard circle equation**, **completing the
square**, the **focus-directrix definition** of the parabola, and the
**tangent formula**. Now we name them.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Circle.** Locus of points at distance $r$ from a fixed centre $(h, k)$.

Standard form:
$$ (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2. $$

General form:
$$ x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, $$
with centre $(-g, -f)$ and radius $\sqrt{g^2 + f^2 - c}$ (provided
$g^2 + f^2 - c > 0$).

**Tangent at $(x_1, y_1)$ on $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$:** $x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2$.

**Parabola.** Locus of points equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and
a fixed line (directrix).

| Equation | Vertex | Focus | Directrix | Opens |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| $y^2 = 4px$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(p, 0)$ | $x = -p$ | right ($p > 0$) |
| $y^2 = -4px$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(-p, 0)$ | $x = p$ | left |
| $x^2 = 4py$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(0, p)$ | $y = -p$ | up |
| $x^2 = -4py$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(0, -p)$ | $y = p$ | down |

**Latus rectum** of $y^2 = 4px$: chord through focus perpendicular to
axis, length $|4p|$.

:::

A short verse:

> *Circle: square of distance equals $r$ squared.*
> *Parabola: distance to point matches distance to line.*
> *Complete the square тАФ the centre slides;*
> *Match $4p$ тАФ and the focus aligns.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the equation of the circle that passes through $(2, 3)$ and has
> centre on the line $y = x + 1$, with radius $5$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Centre $(h, h + 1)$ for some $h$.
2. Distance from $(h, h+1)$ to $(2, 3)$ equals $5$:
   $(h - 2)^2 + (h + 1 - 3)^2 = 25$.
3. $(h - 2)^2 + (h - 2)^2 = 25 \Rightarrow 2(h - 2)^2 = 25 \Rightarrow (h-2)^2 = 25/2 \Rightarrow h = 2 \pm 5/\sqrt{2}$.
4. Two circles, with centres $(2 + 5/\sqrt{2}, 3 + 5/\sqrt{2})$ and
   $(2 - 5/\sqrt{2}, 3 - 5/\sqrt{2})$, both of radius $5$.

**Answer:** two valid circles, with the centres above.

### Practice тАФ Warm

**W1.** Equation of the circle centred at origin, radius $3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x^2 + y^2 = 9$.
</details>

**W2.** Find the centre and radius of $x^2 + y^2 + 4x - 6y + 4 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$g = 2, f = -3, c = 4$. Centre $(-2, 3)$, radius $\sqrt{4 + 9 - 4} = 3$.
</details>

**W3.** Focus and directrix of $y^2 = 12x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4p = 12 \Rightarrow p = 3$. Focus $(3, 0)$, directrix $x = -3$.
</details>

### Practice тАФ Working

**P1.** Find the equation of the circle that has $(1, 1)$ and $(5, 7)$
as the endpoints of a diameter.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Centre = midpoint. Radius = half the diameter length.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Centre $(3, 4)$. Radius $= \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{13}$.

Equation: $(x - 3)^2 + (y - 4)^2 = 13$.
</details>

**P2.** Find the equation of the parabola with vertex at $(0, 0)$,
focus on the $y$-axis, passing through $(2, 4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
$x^2 = 4py$. Plug in $(2, 4)$ to find $p$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4 = 4p \cdot 4 \Rightarrow 4 = 16p \Rightarrow p = 1/4$.

Equation: $x^2 = y$.
</details>

**P3.** Find the length of the chord of the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 25$ cut
off by the line $3x + 4y - 5 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Use the perpendicular-from-centre approach: chord length $= 2\sqrt{r^2 - d^2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Distance from origin to line: $d = |{-5}|/\sqrt{9 + 16} = 1$.

Chord length $= 2\sqrt{25 - 1} = 2\sqrt{24} = 4\sqrt{6}$.
</details>

### Practice тАФ Stretch

**S1.** Find the equation of the circle that passes through the origin
and cuts intercepts of $a$ on the $x$-axis and $b$ on the $y$-axis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Three points on the circle: $(0,0), (a, 0), (0, b)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
General: $x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0$.

At $(0,0)$: $c = 0$.

At $(a, 0)$: $a^2 + 2ga = 0 \Rightarrow g = -a/2$.

At $(0, b)$: $b^2 + 2fb = 0 \Rightarrow f = -b/2$.

Equation: $x^2 + y^2 - ax - by = 0$.
</details>

**S2.** A parabola has its vertex at the origin and passes through the
point $(2, 3)$. The axis of the parabola is along the $x$-axis. Find
its equation and the coordinates of the focus.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y^2 = 4px$. At $(2, 3)$: $9 = 8p \Rightarrow p = 9/8$.

Equation: $y^2 = \dfrac{9}{2} x$.

Focus: $(p, 0) = (9/8, 0)$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The radius of the circle $x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y + 4 = 0$ is
(a) $1$ (b) $2$ (c) $3$ (d) $4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$g = -2, f = 3, c = 4$. Radius $= \sqrt{4 + 9 - 4} = 3$. **Answer: (c).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the focus and directrix of the parabola
$x^2 = 16y$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4p = 16 \Rightarrow p = 4$. Focus $(0, 4)$, directrix $y = -4$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Find the equation of the circle through
$(2, 3)$ and concentric with $x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 5 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Concentric: same centre. Find centre, then compute the new radius.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Centre of given circle: $(2, -3)$. Distance from $(2, -3)$ to $(2, 3)$: $6$.

So new radius $= 6$. Equation: $(x - 2)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 36$, or
$x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 23 = 0$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** A parabolic dish for a satellite has its
cross-section described by $y^2 = 8x$. (i) Where is the focus?
(ii) The dish is $4$ m wide at the rim. How deep is the dish?
(iii) Where should the receiver be placed?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
The receiver goes at the focus. "Width at the rim" gives the chord
perpendicular to the axis at the depth.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $4p = 8 \Rightarrow p = 2$. Focus at $(2, 0)$.

**(ii)** Width at depth $x$: $y$ ranges from $-\sqrt{8x}$ to
$+\sqrt{8x}$, so width $= 2\sqrt{8x}$. Set $2\sqrt{8x} = 4 \Rightarrow
\sqrt{8x} = 2 \Rightarrow 8x = 4 \Rightarrow x = 1/2$. Depth $= 0.5$ m.

**(iii)** At the focus, $(2, 0)$ тАФ at horizontal distance $2$ m from the
back of the dish along the axis of symmetry.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 10 LA "parabolic reflector"
matches the 5-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** the chord-length-from-distance-to-centre identity
(P3) is JEE Main standard.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$x^2 + y^2 = -4$ is a circle of radius $2i$."** No тАФ it has *no
   real points*. The radius squared must be non-negative.
2. **"$y^2 = 4x$ is the same as $y = 2\sqrt{x}$."** Half-true тАФ the
   square root keeps only the upper branch. The full parabola has both
   $y > 0$ and $y < 0$ branches.
3. **"The directrix is inside the parabola."** It is *outside* тАФ on the
   opposite side from the focus, beyond the vertex.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you derive the standard parabola equation from the
   focus-directrix definition?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you complete the square on a general circle equation in under a
   minute?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)](../10b-conic-sections-ellipse-hyperbola/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0тАУ1 yes:** drill the four parabola orientations and the
  centre-radius extraction by completing the square.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Straight Lines](../09-straight-lines/index.qmd)** тАФ directrix is a
  line; chords are line segments.
- **[Class 10 тАФ Quadratic Equations](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/04-quadratic-equations/index.qmd)** тАФ
  conic equations are second-degree.

### What this unlocks

- **[Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)](../10b-conic-sections-ellipse-hyperbola/index.qmd)** тАФ
  the other two slices of the cone, with two foci instead of one.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 10 тАФ Conic Sections** (NCERT 2023 rationalised).
  Sections 10.1тАУ10.3: circle, parabola тАФ definitions, standard
  equations, properties.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t3.conic-sections`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/conic-sections/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Parabolic projectile motion: a thrown object's
path under gravity is a parabola. Characters' jump trajectories,
artillery shells, and bouncing physics use the equation $y = -\dfrac{g}{2v^2}x^2 + \dfrac{v_y}{v_x}x$.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Engineering.** Parabolic mirrors focus parallel rays to a
single point тАФ used in solar concentrators, car headlights, satellite
dishes. The reflective property is a consequence of the
focus-directrix definition.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (the cone-slice intuition for conics), Russian
school (Discover problems force the focus-directrix definition before
the equation), Bh─Бskara (verse on completing-the-square shifting the
centre).
:::
